lesson.menu_desc: Persuasive Language in Supplier Negotiations -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Making Your Case -- Persuasive Language in Supplier Negotiations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Fabiana Michelly Silva | Aula 6 -- Making Your Case

listenings.text: Fabiana, thank you for making time for this call. I want to discuss the Q4 contract renewal. What I would like to propose is a revised pricing structure. It is the volume commitment that makes this deal viable for both sides. What we need from Bloomberg is a guaranteed minimum order per quarter. In return, what we can offer is a fifteen percent reduction on freight costs and priority allocation during peak season. It is not the price that concerns me, it is the unpredictability of order volumes. If we can agree on a framework, what I suggest is that we lock in the terms for twelve months. The reason I am pushing for this is that our production planning depends on stable demand forecasts.

Hi team, quick debrief on the TechFlow negotiation. Overall, it went well. What I focused on was building a case around volume commitment rather than price alone. It was the freight reduction that really got their attention, not the discount on unit price. What surprised me was how open Klaus was to a twelve-month framework. The reason this matters is that it gives us predictability for our supply chain planning. What I recommend we do next is prepare a formal proposal with the exact volume thresholds. It is the specifics that will close this deal, not vague promises. I have asked Marcus to run the numbers, and what we need from finance is approval on the minimum order commitment before Friday.

extra_audio.text: Fabiana opens the negotiation with TechFlow. She proposes a revised pricing structure based on volume. Klaus responds with a freight reduction offer. They discuss a twelve-month framework agreement. Fabiana debriefs the team and recommends preparing a formal proposal.
